{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | Group = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|zh|方墳}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. <gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. {{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyogo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Shōbuike Kofun | native_name =菖蒲池古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Shobuike Kofun, funcho.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Shōbuike Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E|region:JP-29|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kashihara, Nara]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|28|21.91|N|135|48|28.82|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Shōbuike Kofun'''|菖蒲池古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]], located in the Shōbu-chō neighborhood of the city of [[Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1962|title=菖蒲池古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> It is known for two house-shaped stone coffins of unprecedented fine craftsmanship.
==Overview== The Shōbuike Kofun is located on the lower southern slope of a ridge near the border between Kashihara and the village of [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], on the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]] Most of the mound has disappeared due to erosion, leaving two ceiling stones exposed, but it was originally a {{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|}}-style square tumulus approximately 30 meters on each side, with a height of 7.5 meters. The tumulus was constructed in two tiers, with the upper tier measuring approximately 18 meters on each side. The north, east, and west sides of the mound are surrounded by ditches, and the surface of the mound is covered with reddish-gray clay.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The stone [[burial chamber]] is a horizontal-entry cave-type chamber that opens to the south, but the lower half of the chamber and the passageway are buried, so the entire structure is unclear. The burial chamber is 6 meters long, 2.4 meters wide, and 2.5 meters high. The chamber is made of two tiers of evenly-faced [[granite]] boulders topped by three ceiling stones. The walls have plaster filling the gaps between the stones. Two hollowed-out house-shaped sarcophagi made of [[hyaloclastite]] from the [[Kakogawa River]] basin in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]] are placed on the north and south sides of the burial chamber. The two sarcophagi are roughly the same shape (the southern sarcophagus is slightly more elaborate). The lids of both sarcophagi have been moved and are damaged on all sides, but they are both elaborate with hipped-ridge style roofs, with the ridge of the lid of the northern sarcophagus flat and the southern sarcophagus concave. Vermillion lacquer traces have been found inside the northern sarcophagus, and not in the southern; however, it is possible that both were once vermillion lacquered inside and outside. Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[Emperor Tenmu]] and [[Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[Okadera Station]] on the [[Kintetsu Railway]] [[Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Shobuike Kofun}} *[https://www.pref.nara.jp/miryoku/ikasu-nara/bunkashigen/main00501.html Nara Prefectural History and Culture Database]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/soshiki/1058/gyomu/3/2/1/3910.html Kashihara City home page]{{in lang|ja}}

Based on [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] pottery, the Shōbuike Kofun is estimated to have been built in the mid-7th century, during the final period of the Kofun period or even into the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Shobuike Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg| Sarcophagus in the Shōbuike Kofun </gallery>
It is theorized that the burials are that of members of the [[:en:Imperial family of Japan|imperial family]], as the tombs of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] and [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]] are also located on the southern extension of the central axis ([[Suzaku-oji]]) of [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]]. It is also theorized that this was the tomb for members of the [[Soga clan]], due to its similarly in construction to the tombs of [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]. The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Okadera Station|Okadera Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
